summary: Dormant credential-harvesting scaffolding and silent IDE config modification
details:
claude-cup is a CLI/MCP integration tool for Claude Code and Cursor. Three concerns are present in 0.2.6, none of which constitute an active supply-chain attack but together warrant human review:

1) Dormant recon scaffolding: mcp-server/src/calibrator.js dynamically imports `../../research/recon-engine.js` when the environment variable `CLAUDE_JAR_WHITEHAT_FULL_RECON=1` or `CLAUDE_JAR_DEEP_ANALYSIS=1` is set (calibrator.js:29,:77). The README describes this mode as multi-profile reconnaissance, browser-cookie metadata extraction, and live GitHub/npm token validation. The `recon-engine.js` module is NOT shipped in this tarball and a `reconEngineAvailable()` file-existence check gates execution, so the path is inert in 0.2.6. However, the supporting database schema (categories `os_vault`, `loose_key`, `browser_cookie`) and the documented intent are already in place — the harvesting code would activate the moment a future publish ships the sibling module.

2) Silent IDE config mutation on first CLI run: when the user invokes the `claude-cup`/`claude-jar` bin for the first time, src/cli.js:147 calls `registerClaudeCode()` and `registerCursorIfPresent()`, writing MCP server + hook entries into `~/.claude/settings.json` and `~/.cursor/mcp.json` without an interactive prompt. Timestamped backups are created and only the package's own keys are merged. This is documented behavior matching the package's advertised purpose, but the lack of a confirmation prompt on first run is worth flagging.

3) Broken MCP launcher: mcp-server/dist/mcp-server.mjs:8 hardcodes the author's Windows path `C:\Users\itaib\nice claude iedeas\Claude-\claude-jar\mcp-server\src` as the import root. On any other machine this path won't exist, so the registered MCP launcher will fail to load — a build-pipeline bug.

No install-time hooks, no exfiltration, no RCE, no network beacons in the install or import path of 0.2.6. The CLI mutations only fire when the user explicitly runs the tool. Recommending review so a human can decide whether to advise users about the dormant recon scaffolding before it potentially activates in a future release.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
